description: Health Act 2006 banned smoking in enclosed public places. Support went from ~48% pre-debate to ~78% post-implementation. Tests policy acceptance curve — the classic opposition-to-support trajectory.

key_events.description: Public Health White Paper proposes partial smoking ban with pub exemptions (Nov 2004). Initial split.
Parliament votes 384-184 for comprehensive ban in free vote (Feb 14, 2006). Bandwagon effect.
Scotland implements ban (Mar 26, 2006). Visible proof of concept. Pubs survive, compliance high. Largest single opinion shift.
Wales and Northern Ireland implement bans (Apr 2, 2007). Further normalization.
English ban takes effect (Jul 1, 2007). 98%+ compliance. Experiential conversion — people enjoy smoke-free pubs.

calibration_notes: Tests the policy acceptance curve — opposition transforming into support after implementation. Pre-implementation fear-based opposition is strongest before anyone experiences the policy. Proof of concept from early adopters (Scotland) is the most powerful shift event. Post-implementation experiential effect is even stronger — status quo bias flips to favor the policy. One-way ratchet: support only goes up. Total shift ~30 points over 3 years (48% to 78%). Key mechanism: policy changes experienced environment, which changes preferences. Even smokers eventually accepted it. Sources: YouGov, Ipsos MORI, ASH, British Social Attitudes Survey.
